nodes.text: Chapter 8
She stood at the counter for a count of about thirty.

She had said Roy in a voice that was not the voice she had been using with him. She had pushed back. The pushing back had felt good at the time. It also gave Roy a piece of information he had not had before walking in, which was that she could be made to push back. Tem had told them yesterday: the danger is being known by the wrong thing.
She had let the wrong thing know her this morning a little better than it had known her at eleven-fifty-eight.
She picked up her phone and called Tem. It rang four times. Tem picked up on the fifth.
Lena.
Roy was just in the bookstore.
A beat of silence on the other end.
What did he ask.

She told her. The three book titles — The Architecture of Inland Water, Pattern Persistence: Selected Essays, The Seven Floors of the Glass House. The Soren question. The Tem question. The Jorgen question. The line about being up at Jorgen's in June and buying a small one for the wife.
A long pause.
He knows about the Jorgen correspondence.
Yes.
How does he know.
I don't know. Hannah at the Portland gallery, possibly. Jorgen himself, possibly. Petra, less possibly. Possibly nobody — possibly Roy is guessing because Roy reads people and reads them well.
He saw me react. I gave it to him.
You gave him a half-second. He probably already had it. The half-second was confirmation. Don't carry it too hard.
A breath on the line.
How are you, Lena.

The question landed in a way Lena did not expect. She had been at the bookstore for five hours running a Monday alone, had handled three customers and a hold and a child crying in the doorway and a man whose existence in her shop she had been managing for two and a half years walking in and asking her about her private correspondence, and Tem on the other end of the line was asking how she was, and she did not know. She had not asked herself. She had not been doing the kind of thinking that included that question.
I don't know yet.

I'm coming down. The boys are still up here. Cale's been talking. Kai's at the table. Finn's on the porch. I have what I need from Cale for now. I'll be at the bookstore by two. Stay until Petra's back. Then come up to my apartment and we'll wait for the boys to come down.
All right.
One more thing. Don't open the door for Roy if he comes back. If he comes back, lock it from the inside and call me.
All right.
She hung up. She looked at the bell over the door for a long count.
Mrs. Marsden's husband came in at twelve-twenty for a book he had ordered. Lena gave it to him. He paid in tokens.

A woman she did not know came in at one-fifteen and asked for the Inga monograph. Lena pulled it from the office hold, took the cash, wrote the name down in the hold ledger as Petra had taught her to do.

A drone passed low over Hemlock at one-thirty-five, north to south, doing whatever it was doing at that hour. Lena registered the drone the way she registered drones, which was as a thing that was always somewhere in the sky. The drone went past and was gone.
Tem came in at one-fifty-eight.

She did not, when she came in, kiss Lena. She did not put a hand anywhere on Lena. She came up to the counter and stood across it from Lena with her hands flat on the wood between them and looked at her for a long count, and the looking was the form of touching the bookstore allowed.
Petra.
Should be back by three.
I'll wait with you.
All right.

Tem took her coat off and hung it on the hook by the office door where Petra had had hers hanging this morning. She came back to the counter and pulled the small stool Lena used when she was alone in the shop around to her side and sat on it.
For a while neither of them spoke. The cat in the front window opened one eye and looked at Tem and closed it.
What did Cale say at the cabin this morning.
Tem did not, for a beat, answer.
He said her name.
Whose.
Aria.

The word landed the way a word lands when you have been near it for some time without knowing it had been there. Lena did not, in the moment, know what to do with it. She picked up the small brass bell on the counter and turned it over in her hand and set it back down.

Brandon told him in 2050. Cale held the name for two years. He said it this morning at the cabin in front of Kai and Finn. He hadn't planned to. It came out. He said he was sorry for the way it came out and Kai said don't be sorry, it came out, that's all right. Then Lena — then we sat with it. Then you called.
Aria.
Aria.
The bell rang.

Petra came in with rain on her hair and the small canvas tote bag she carried up the river full of papers with mud on them. She saw the two of them at the counter, the cup of cold coffee in front of Tem, the brass bell upended on the wood, and did not say anything for a count. She set the tote bag on the table by the window display. She came up to the counter and put a hand on Lena's hand on the counter.
All right. Talk to me.
They talked to her.
Outside, the rain did what it had been doing.
The cat slept.
